articles.title: Don't Be Alarmed
articles.dek: A young man fell three stories during Paul's longest sermon. The church's response that night reveals something about what a resurrection community actually looks like.
articles.quote: He fell among people who knew what to do with death.
articles.body:
Picture a Friday night gathering in a barangay chapel somewhere in Negros Occidental. The plastic chairs have been pushed back to make room. The ceiling fan is on its highest setting but the air is still thick with that pressing, humid heat that makes breathing feel like work. The service started at eight. It is now past ten. The pastor is deep in the middle of a teaching, and the young man near the window — maybe seventeen, maybe eighteen, someone's kuya or younger brother — is fighting the most ancient battle in the history of church attendance. His head dips. He catches himself. His head dips again. You have seen this person. On a particularly warm Sunday, you may have been this person.

His name is Eutychus. He is in Acts 20, and what happens to him is one of the most vivid, most breathlessly human moments in the entire New Testament.

Paul is in Troas, a port city on the western coast of what is now Turkey. He is gathered with the local believers in an upstairs room — three floors up, lit by many oil lamps, Acts 20:8 notes, which would have made the air even thicker, even warmer. The night is long. Paul is heading toward Jerusalem in the morning, and he does what every departing pastor does when he suspects this might be the last time: he talks. And talks. Acts 20:7 tells us the gathering has already stretched past midnight.

Young Eutychus — his name means fortunate in Greek, which is one of those quiet details that only lands later — is sitting on the windowsill. This is not laziness. This is survival: the window is where the night air comes in, where you have at least a fighting chance against the warmth and the late hour. He is not a bad disciple. He is a tired human being, doing his honest best in an impossible situation.

And then he falls asleep.

Three floors down.

Acts 20:9 does not soften it: "Sinking into a deep sleep as Paul talked on and on, he fell to the ground from the third story and was picked up dead."

Imagine the room. The sudden silence. The gasp. Thirty or forty people pouring down narrow stairs and into the street below, torches in hand, finding the boy's body on the cobblestones. This is the kind of moment that ends a gathering. This is the kind of tragedy that becomes the story people tell for years — remember the night in Troas? Remember the boy who fell? This is where the service, and probably the young church in Troas, should shatter.

But Paul does not let it become that story.

He pushes through the crowd and goes to the boy. What Paul does next carries the deliberate echo of two older prophets: he throws himself over Eutychus, the same way Elijah once draped himself over the son of the widow of Zarephath, the same way Elisha stretched himself across the Shunammite's boy. He does not make a speech. He does not raise his hands to the crowd. He holds the child. And then he speaks — three words in the original Greek:

"Don't be alarmed," he said, "he's alive!" — Acts 20:10 (NIV)

Present tense. Calm. No ceremony.

Here is what I keep turning over when I sit with this passage: Paul does not make this miracle the climax of the night. He does not give the benediction, let everyone go home stunned and grateful, and write a letter about what God did in Troas. He goes back upstairs. He breaks bread. He eats with his friends. And then — Acts 20:11 — he keeps talking until the sun comes up.

The miracle is not the centerpiece. The fellowship is the centerpiece. The resurrection is almost a parenthesis — a necessary, unhurried correction — and then the community simply continues. As if death had been an interruption, and not a full stop.

This is worth sitting with for a long moment. We live in a world that treats tragedy as a period. Something terrible happens and everything pauses — the gathering dissolves, the momentum dies, the plan sits on a shelf indefinitely while everyone waits to feel normal again. We are very practiced at letting interruptions become endings. We are much less practiced at the thing Paul models here: going back upstairs after something has been set right, and simply continuing in bread and Word and the warm company of people we love.

There is a name for a community that can do this. It's called a resurrection community. Not a community that is immune to shock or horror — the crowd's terror when Eutychus hits the ground is real, and it matters. But a community that understands, in its bones, that death does not get to end the gathering.

Maybe your community — your family, your barkada, your marriage, your small group — has had a Eutychus moment. Someone fell. Something fell. The air went out of the room and the gathering scattered, and nobody has been brave enough to call everyone back together. You have been waiting, perhaps, for things to feel normal before picking up where you left off.

This story gives you permission to stop waiting. Paul did not hold out for grief to fully process before going back to the table. He held the boy, said he's alive, and went back upstairs — because he understood something we easily miss: the table and the Word are not what you do after healing. They are how the healing travels. The fellowship is the medicine, not the reward for surviving the crisis.

Acts 20:12 is easy to rush past, but don't: "The people took the young man home alive and were greatly comforted." Not just comforted because Eutychus was breathing. Comforted because they had all — together — gone back upstairs and stayed until the sun came up. The staying was part of the miracle.

Eutychus means fortunate. He went home that night without fully understanding how accurately his name described him — not because he survived a three-story fall, but because he fell among people who knew what to do with death. People who picked him up, said three words, went back upstairs, and refused to stop.

You and I are just as fortunate. Whatever has fallen — whatever long night, whatever high window, whatever moment of sheer exhaustion brought us down — we belong to the same God whose first instinct when he finds a dead body is to throw himself over it.

Don't be alarmed. He's alive.

That is the gospel in a single scene. Go back upstairs. Break bread. Keep talking until the dawn comes.

articles.title: The Full Price
articles.dek: David had just presided over his worst mistake as king. When a stranger offered him everything he needed for free, he refused — and his reason reveals something most of us miss about what love and worship really are.
articles.quote: I will not take for the LORD what is yours, or sacrifice a burnt offering that costs me nothing.
articles.body:
Picture your Tita Baby — the one in the family who lives three provinces away. Every reunion, she insists on driving down to bring her homemade leche flan, the one she has been preparing since Tuesday. You have told her a hundred times: "Tita, don't bother, we'll just order from Red Ribbon, it's fine." And every time, she gives you that look — not quite offended, but close. Because she understands something you are still learning. The store-bought flan isn't the point. The three days in the kitchen, the early morning drive, the love she poured into the mold — that is the point. If it costs her nothing, it means nothing.

David understood this. But he had to learn it the hard way — at the end of his worst chapter.

First Chronicles 21 opens with a sentence that should make us pause: "Satan rose up against Israel and incited David to take a census of Israel." (1 Chr 21:1) David wanted to count his fighting men — not out of gratitude, but out of pride. His general Joab pushed back hard: don't do this. David overruled him. The census was taken.

God's displeasure was swift. A plague swept through Israel. Seventy thousand people died. And David, watching the destroying angel stretch its hand toward Jerusalem, fell facedown before God — not to negotiate, not to explain himself, but to confess. "I am the one who has sinned and done wrong," he said. "These are but sheep. What have they done? Lord my God, let your hand fall on me and my family, but do not let this plague remain on your people." (1 Chr 21:17)

This is David at his most human. Not the warrior-king. Not the poet. Just a man who broke something he could not fix, watching innocent people pay for it, and putting himself in between. He doesn't point to Satan's role. He doesn't cite the pressures of leadership. He owns it entirely. He stands in front of his people and says: if anyone pays for this, it should be me.

God's response, given through the prophet Gad, was both merciful and specific: go up and build an altar on the threshing floor of Araunah the Jebusite. So David went.

Araunah saw the king approaching and bowed to the ground. Then he made a lavish offer: "Take it! Let my lord the king do whatever pleases him. Look, I will give the oxen for the burnt offerings, the threshing sledges for the wood, and the wheat for the grain offering. I will give all this." (1 Chr 21:23)

Everything David needed, offered freely. By a generous man who clearly respected the king. Take it, David. It is already yours for the asking.

David said no.

"No, I insist on paying the full price. I will not take for the LORD what is yours, or sacrifice a burnt offering that costs me nothing." — 1 Chronicles 21:24

Notice what he does not say. He doesn't say "it's a matter of principle" or "there are rules about royal offerings." He says: I will not sacrifice to God what costs me nothing. As if an offering without cost is not really an offering at all. As if the price itself is part of what makes it worship.

This is the line worth sitting with. David had just presided over the deaths of seventy thousand people because of his pride. He had wept, confessed, and received God's mercy. He is not performing generosity here. He is responding — from somewhere deep inside — to the weight of what this moment means. And what he understands, in his gut, is this: love that looks for the free option isn't really love. Worship that costs nothing reveals a heart that values nothing. The discount, in this case, would have been the insult.

He paid six hundred shekels of gold for the threshing floor. Every shekel meant something.

From that ground, everything changed. David declared: "The house of the LORD God is to be here, and also the altar of burnt offering for Israel." (1 Chr 22:1) The threshing floor bought at full price — the site of a plague's end, the place where David fell facedown and meant it — became the site of Solomon's Temple. Sacred ground, purchased the expensive way, on purpose.

Then came harder news. God told David he would not be the one to build the Temple. He had shed too much blood in war. That honor would fall to his son Solomon — a man of peace. David could have been crushed. He could have stepped back and handed Solomon an empty assignment, the spiritual equivalent of passing a half-finished project to a colleague and walking out. Instead, he did something that reveals the full measure of his character. He spent his remaining years gathering iron and bronze beyond counting, cedar logs without number, gold and silver by the hundred thousand talents — and handed it all to Solomon before his death. "I have taken great pains to provide for the temple of the LORD," he told his son. (1 Chr 22:14)

A father who worked harder for something he would never see completed than most people work for their own dreams. A man who poured everything into his son's calling, knowing the dedication day was not his to attend. He did not negotiate a smaller gift. He did not give Solomon a starter kit and a prayer. He gave everything he had left. That is love at full price.

Most of us have had our Araunah moment — that point where someone offers us the shortcut. "Don't worry, it's fine, you don't have to." And we have to decide: is our love real enough to cost us something, or are we quietly relieved to take the free option?

It shows up in small places, the ones nobody will notice. The apology that is quick and cheap versus the one that requires sitting across from someone, looking them in the eyes, and saying the hard thing out loud. The gift chosen carefully because you know the person's taste versus the one grabbed at the last minute because you were too busy. The prayer that is an actual conversation with God — unhurried, honest, costly in its attention — versus one that costs you nothing emotionally. The Saturday morning given to a child who just wants your presence, not your productivity. The quiet, unglamorous work you pour into someone else's future — a younger sibling, a student, a team member — knowing you may never see the result.

David's word is simple and searching: I will not offer to God what costs me nothing. You can let that line quietly audit your life today. Where are you taking the free option — in your relationship with God, with the people you love, with the calling you have been given?

The good news is not that David was extraordinarily holy. He wasn't — he had just made one of the gravest mistakes of his reign, and seventy thousand people bore the consequences. The good news is that in the rubble of his failure, David discovered what real worship looks like. He came to the threshing floor broken and left with something more valuable than the land: clarity. He knew what love looked like. And he spent every remaining year of his life living it out — for God, for his son, for Israel.

He paid six hundred shekels of gold for a plot of ground. From that ground, the Temple rose. The cost was the point.

What ground in your life is God asking you to buy at full price today?

articles.title: No Footprints in the Water
articles.dek: God opened a path through the sea, walked His people through, and closed the waters behind them — leaving not a single footprint. That is not absence. That is how He moves.
articles.quote: Your path led through the sea, your way through the mighty waters, though your footprints were not seen. — Psalm 77:19, NIV
articles.body:
The morning after Typhoon Carina swept through Quezon province, a fisherman named Mang Totoy walked down to the beach before anyone else was awake. He half expected to find his world rearranged. He was right. His bangka was split in half two hundred meters down the shore. The nipa hut beside the water had no roof. The barangay road sat under half a meter of sand. But the water itself — the same water that had churned all night with waves that woke the whole barangay — was flat, silver, and still. Completely calm. As if nothing had happened at all.

Something enormous had moved through this place. Something that rearranged everything it touched. And then the sea looked like it had never been disturbed.

Asaph, the worship leader who wrote Psalm 77, is drowning on dry land. He cannot sleep. He cries out to God and hears nothing back. “I cried out to God for help; I cried out to God to hear me,” he writes (Ps 77:1, NIV). He wonders if God has forgotten him — “Has his unfailing love vanished forever?” (Ps 77:8, NIV). His hands are stretched out in the night. His soul refuses comfort. Anyone who has prayed into a silence that seemed to echo back empty knows exactly where Asaph is sitting.

But then something shifts. Asaph stops staring at his present pain and starts looking backward — at what God has already done. He remembers the Exodus. He remembers the night God walked Israel out of Egypt and through the Red Sea on dry ground. And what he finds in that memory changes everything about how he sees his silence.

Picture it with him. The Israelites have left Egypt. They are free — or they think they are. Then they turn around and see Pharaoh’s army closing in behind them: six hundred chariots, horses, trained soldiers. In front of them, the Red Sea. There is nowhere to go. Panic fills the camp. People are shouting at Moses — “Was it because there were no graves in Egypt that you brought us to the desert to die?” (Ex 14:11, NIV). Moses tells them to stand still and watch God work.

Then God moves.

Asaph describes it the way you describe something you can barely find words for. The sea saw God and writhed. The clouds poured down water, the heavens resounded with thunder, and God’s arrows flashed back and forth (Ps 77:17, NIV). The earth trembled and quaked (Ps 77:18, NIV). This was not a quiet miracle. This was the deep shuddering under the weight of the Almighty. Every man, woman, child, and animal crossed over on ground that had been underwater minutes before. The waters stood like walls on either side. They walked between them — and made it through.

Then the waters came back. The army drowned. The sea went still.

“Your path led through the sea, your way through the mighty waters, though your footprints were not seen.” (Psalm 77:19, NIV)

No footprints. No groove carved into the ocean floor. No mark in the sand. If you came to that same spot the next morning and looked out at the water, you would see nothing unusual. The surface was calm. The sea looked like it had never been touched.

Here is what is worth sitting with today: God performed the greatest rescue in Israel’s history and left no physical evidence behind.

He didn’t leave a scar on the seabed. He didn’t carve a monument you could revisit. He didn’t even leave muddy footprints on the sand the way you would find at any beach in Batangas or La Union after a crowd has passed through. The sea simply closed. And if you hadn’t been there, if you hadn’t crossed it yourself, you would look at that water and see nothing remarkable at all.

Think about it this way: when you walk on wet sand, your footprints last maybe a minute before the tide erases them. But you were there. The ground held your weight. The moment was real. God’s path through the Red Sea was real too — and then the water took it back. This is how God often works: not through monuments we build to preserve the evidence, but through lives He carries to the other side.

Notice what this means: the absence of visible evidence is not the same as the absence of God.

We live in a time that trusts what it can measure. If God was here, we think, there should be a mark. A sign. Something we can photograph and point to. But Asaph is saying something different. He is saying that the God who commands the heavens and shakes the earth is also the God who works in ways that leave no fingerprints — not because He was absent, but because the sea was never meant to hold His footprints. Water doesn’t keep impressions. The miracle isn’t the footprint. The miracle is that the people got to the other side.

Think about the seasons in your own life when you could not see God working. The illness that lifted slowly, with no single dramatic moment. The relationship that healed over years — one quiet conversation at a time, one shared meal, one late-night call that changed something small and then everything. The financial pressure that eased, and you’re not even sure exactly when the weight started to lift. The child who wandered, and then one day, came home. You look back now and you cannot point to the exact moment and say: there — that is where God put His hand on it. But you are on the other side. The sea is behind you. And it has closed.

Filipino faith has always known this, even without putting it in those words. You see it in the way your lola lights a candle — not because she expects a lightning bolt, but because she trusts that the God she cannot see is doing what she cannot do. You see it in the way Filipino families carry hardship: not by demanding an explanation first, but by keeping going, holding each other, praying anyway. That quiet, persistent trust is not weakness. It is the ancient posture of people who have been living inside Psalm 77 for three thousand years without knowing it.

Asaph does not end his psalm with an explanation of why God seemed absent. He ends it with the memory of the crossing — and with this: “You led your people like a flock by the hand of Moses and Aaron” (Ps 77:20, NIV). A shepherd does not explain to the sheep where the path goes. He just walks ahead. And the sheep who trust find that they have crossed what they could not have crossed alone.

If you are in a season right now where God feels invisible — where you have prayed and heard silence, where you have looked for a sign and found only still water — Asaph has something for you today. Go back to the last time you crossed. Not the last time you felt inspired at church. The last time something in your life was impossible, and then you were on the other side of it, and you could not fully explain how.

That was the path through the sea. That was God’s footprint in the water — gone now, but real.

The God who moves through storms without leaving a mark is still the God who gets His people to the other side. He was leading then. He is leading now. And the fact that you cannot see His footprints in your present moment does not mean He has stopped walking. It might simply mean the water hasn’t settled yet.

articles.title: Every Bird Can See It
articles.dek: The gang in Proverbs 1 thought they were the hunters. Solomon says they were the prey — and every bird overhead already knew it.
articles.quote: Every trap the greedy set is baited with their own life.
articles.body:
If you grew up in the province, you might remember watching a lolo or older cousin set a bird trap. A flat piece of wood propped up by a forked twig, a string tied to the twig, a handful of rice scattered underneath. The plan is simple: the bird lands, pecks at the grain, nudges the stick — and the board falls. You'd crouch behind the tall grass and wait. Sometimes for a very long time. Because most birds, it turns out, can see a trap from fifty meters away. They land nearby, tilt their head, look at the propped-up board and the obvious bait — and fly away. Every bird can see it.

This week, the Office of the Ombudsman filed plunder charges against a sitting senator, alleging he failed to declare P75 million in campaign donations. Whatever the courts ultimately decide, the image is familiar: someone who believed they were being clever with money, now standing in the full light of public scrutiny. Proverbs 1 was written three thousand years ago, but Solomon had this exact kind of scene in mind.

Solomon is speaking to his son — young, impressionable, trying to find his footing in a world full of competing voices. And there is a gang of men in the street with a pitch ready. They have thought this through. "Come along with us," they say in verse 11. "Let's lie in wait for innocent blood... we will get all sorts of valuable things and fill our houses with plunder. Throw in your lot with us, and we will share a common purse." (Proverbs 1:11–14, NIV) Read those words out loud. It is the oldest sales pitch on earth. Easy money. Shared risk. Brotherhood. Split the gains, and if anything goes wrong, no one person takes the fall. The appeal is not just greed — it is belonging. The gang offers community, a common purpose, the intoxicating feeling that you are finally on the inside of something real.

Solomon does not just say "stay away." He says: look carefully at what these men actually are. "Their feet rush into evil," he tells his son, "they are swift to shed blood." (Proverbs 1:16, NIV) These are not clever entrepreneurs bending the rules. They are men who are running — running toward their own ruin — and they cannot see it. And then Solomon says something that should stop us cold.

"How useless to spread a net where every bird can see it! But these people lie in wait for their own blood; they ambush only themselves!" (Proverbs 1:17–18, NIV)

Read that again slowly. These men think they are the hunters. They have a scheme, a plan, a common purse. But Solomon, watching from a higher vantage, sees what they cannot see about themselves: they are not the hunters. They are the prey. Every net they spread is baited with their own lives. Every ambush they lay is waiting for them at the end of the very path they have chosen.

The bird in verse 17 is smarter than the sinner. The bird sees the propped-up wood and flies away. But the man who chases ill-gotten gain walks straight into the trap he built with his own hands. This is not just poetry. It is a description of how sin actually works. Every scheme to enrich yourself at someone else's expense carries, embedded within it, the seed of your own destruction. Greed does not just risk discovery — it reshapes the man who practices it. Corruption does not just threaten your future — it hollows out your present. "It takes away the life of those who get it," Solomon concludes in verse 19 (NIV). The trap always catches the trapper first.

It is worth asking: why can't the sinner see what every bird sees? Because the bird has no appetite for the grain. It can look at the trap clearly because it has no stake in the bait. The sinner's own desire blinds him to the danger. He sees the grain and everything else blurs. The gang, the brotherhood, the common purse — these are the grain. And once a man's eyes are fixed on what he wants, the propped-up stick and the string and the loaded board simply disappear from view. This is exactly why Solomon devotes so much of chapter one to urging his son to choose wisdom before the invitation arrives — because once desire is lit, judgment goes dark. The best time to decide you will not join the gang is long before they show up at your door, holding out a fistful of easy money and the warmth of belonging.

The "easy money" pitch shows up in every generation, wearing different clothes. Sometimes it is as dramatic as a senator's undeclared millions. More often it is smaller and closer: the padded expense report, the underdeclared income on your tax return, the shortcut that costs the person next to you but benefits you, the group chat where things get shared that really should not be shared. And every time, the pitch sounds reasonable. It sounds like belonging. It sounds like you are finally on the inside. But Solomon is watching from above, and from where he stands, the net is obvious. Every bird can see it.

Here is the grace hidden inside this hard passage. Jesus Christ also walked into an ambush — not because he was naive, but because he chose to. He entered Jerusalem knowing that betrayal, false charges, and a cross were waiting. He saw the trap clearly and walked straight into it, on purpose, for us. And something extraordinary happened: the ambush meant to destroy him became the hinge of all history. The net set against him became the rescue of everyone who had ever been caught in their own trap. The cross that was meant to be his end became our beginning.

You and I are not birds clever enough to avoid all the grain scattered on the ground. We have been caught, at one point or another, in schemes of our own making. But Christ stepped into the net voluntarily — and sprung it from the inside — so that we could walk free from the things that would otherwise take our lives.

So when the invitation comes — and it will come — you do not have to pretend you feel no pull toward the grain. You just need to remember what Solomon saw from his higher vantage: every trap the greedy set is baited with their own life. Bring the invitation into the light before you decide. Talk to someone you trust. Ask what it will cost the person standing next to you. And when you walk away — not because you are stronger or smarter, but because you are loved by One who already paid for the times you did not walk away — you will have understood what Solomon was trying to tell his son all along.

The birds knew. Now you do too.
